question: Where is the containment vessel (CNV) installed?

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, emphasizing the use of nickel-based Alloy 718 for compatibility and stress corrosion cracking mitigation. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspections and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest ASME BPVC Section XI requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. Its primary function is to transport heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, while also providing mechanisms for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module (NPM), which integrates the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV) surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component, supporting various reactor systems and providing a barrier to fission product release, with materials and fabrication standards ensuring integrity throughout the plant's design lifetime.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. Any leakage into containment, regardless of source, vaporizes and disperses quickly, so all leakage is treated as unidentified until specifically located and quantified by other means; leakage rates are determined by RCS inventory balance and compared to containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates. The installed leakage detection systems are reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for reactor components.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism and secondary side corrosion risks considered low.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat following reactor shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours to protect fuel and system integrity, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

edges.description: Section B states that reactor vent valves discharge to the CNV, implying the CNV is installed in a location to receive such discharges from the RPV, while Section A discusses components (threaded fasteners) used in the RPV and related boundaries, connecting both sections through their association with the RPV and its containment.

Section B discusses leakage detection within the containment, implying that the containment vessel (CNV) is the location where such leakage would be detected, thus indirectly indicating the CNV's installation surrounds the RCS components discussed in Section A.

Section A discusses the materials and standards for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary and cites Section B as a reference for quality assurance and material reliability standards.
Section B states that the containment vessel (CNV) surrounds the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), thus specifying the installation location of the CNV, which is not directly described in Section A.

Section A describes the CNV as the destination for discharged steam and gases from the reactor system, while Section B discusses leakage detection within the containment, implying that the CNV is installed as the containment for the reactor system.

Section A describes the function and operation of the CNV in the reactor system, while Section B lists references that provide supporting standards and guidelines for the integrity and quality assurance of reactor components, including the CNV.

Section A states that the containment vessel (CNV) surrounds the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B refers to leakage detection inside the containment, thus both sections connect by describing the physical relationship and functional context of the CNV within the NuScale Power Module.

Section A discusses leakage detection within the containment, while Section B lists references cited in Section A, indicating that Section A relies on the standards and guidelines referenced in Section B for its technical basis.

Section B states that reactor vent valves discharge the pressurizer steam space directly to the CNV, indicating that the CNV is installed in a location accessible to the reactor coolant system and emergency core cooling system.
Section B states that the containment vessel (CNV) surrounds the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) within each NuScale Power Module (NPM), thereby specifying the installation location of the CNV.
